Comparisons
vs Hindsight
  1. Docs
  2. Hindsight

Mnemosyne vs Hindsight

An honest, technical comparison for users running memory systems locally with Hermes Agent and OpenClaw.

The Distinction That Matters

Hindsight has two products:

ProductHostingWhat You Get
Hindsight CloudManaged by Vectorize.ioDashboard, cloud sync, managed inference
Hindsight Self-HostedYour own Docker containerFull control, no cloud dependency, local PostgreSQL

Mnemosyne's previous comparison page conflated these. This page fixes that.

Architecture at a Glance

DimensionMnemosyneHindsight Self-Hosted
Process modelIn-process Python librarySeparate Docker container (FastAPI + PostgreSQL)
IPC overheadZero (function calls)HTTP + JSON serialization
DatabaseSQLite (file-based)PostgreSQL + pgvector (server-based)
Embedding enginefastembed ONNX (67MB BGE-small)sentence-transformers PyTorch (~500MB)
Vector searchsqlite-vec or numpy fallbackpgvector HNSW (mature, optimized)
Cold startInstant (if models cached)~5-10s (container boot)
Memory per query~10-20MB~100-300MB (connection pool + PyTorch)

Memory Model Comparison

Mnemosyne: BEAM (Bilevel Episodic-Associative Memory)

Three tiers:

TierPurposeEviction
Working MemoryHot context, auto-injected into promptsTTL (default 24h) + max 10,000 items
Episodic MemoryLong-term consolidated storageNone (append-only with invalidation)
ScratchpadTemporary agent reasoningMax 1,000 items, explicit clear

Retrieval: Single-pass hybrid scoring: 0.5 * vector + 0.3 * FTS + 0.2 * importance, multiplied by recency decay.

Hindsight: Retain / Recall / Reflect

StageWhat It Does
RetainLLM extracts 2-5 structured facts per chunk, normalizes entities, stores with provenance
Recall4-way parallel retrieval (semantic + BM25 + graph + temporal), fused via RRF, reranked by cross-encoder
ReflectAgentic loop with tool calling, mental models, disposition traits

Feature Matrix

CapabilityMnemosyneHindsight Self-Hosted
Entity resolution\u274c No \u2014 exact string dedup only\u2705 Yes \u2014 spaCy + LLM + trigram matching
Automatic fact extraction\u274c No \u2014 stores raw text\u2705 Yes \u2014 LLM-driven structured facts
Temporal memory\u26a0\ufe0f Manual \u2014 valid_until + superseded_by\u2705 Automatic \u2014 native date parsing, temporal recall strategy
Conflict detection\u274c No \u2014 mark old facts expired\u2705 Yes \u2014 background consolidation with evidence tracking
Cross-machine sharing\u274c No \u2014 same-machine SQLite only\u2705 Yes \u2014 HTTP API on port 8888
Multi-tenancy\u274c No \u2014 session/global binary scope\u2705 Yes \u2014 HindClaw adds JWT/API key auth + per-tenant schemas
Mental models\u274c No\u2705 Yes \u2014 user-curated summaries with delta updates
Graph traversal\u26a0\ufe0f TripleStore only (S-P-O queries)\u2705 Yes \u2014 entity link expansion + co-occurrence tracking
OpenClaw integration\u274c Not yet built\u2705 Native \u2014 openclaw-hindsight plugin exists
Hermes integration\u2705 Native in-process\u2705 Via HTTP client

The Honest Trade-Off

Mnemosyne is simpler, faster, lighter. Hindsight is smarter, more sophisticated, more complete.

Use Mnemosyne if you want:

  • The simplest possible deployment: pip install mnemosyne-memory
  • Fastest recall latency (no HTTP, no PyTorch overhead)
  • Raw-text storage without structured fact extraction
  • Single-machine, single-user operation
  • Something that "just works" without containers
  • Full control over the memory model

Use Hindsight Self-Hosted if you want:

  • Entity resolution ("this person is the same as that person")
  • Automatic fact extraction from raw text
  • Cross-machine memory sharing
  • Multi-tenant memory banks
  • Temporal reasoning ("what happened last week?")
  • Sophisticated retrieval with reranking
  • Docker + PostgreSQL complexity is acceptable

Concrete Scenario: 100-Turn Chat

At turn 90, the agent needs to recall a fact from turn 10.

Hindsight path:

  1. HTTP POST to localhost:8888
  2. 4-way parallel recall with RRF fusion
  3. Cross-encoder reranks results
  4. Returns structured facts with confidence
  5. Latency: ~50-200ms

Mnemosyne path:

  1. Direct Python function call
  2. sqlite-vec vector similarity + FTS5 keyword match
  3. Hybrid score with recency decay
  4. Returns raw text memories
  5. Latency: ~2-10ms for 10K corpus

Where Mnemosyne wins: 5-20\u00d7 faster, ~100MB total footprint vs ~500MB+, no container orchestration.

Where Hindsight wins: Better recall quality, structured facts with provenance, entity-aware retrieval.

Dependency Reality Check

Mnemosyne

ModeDependenciesNetwork Calls
Minimal (keyword only)Python stdlib + SQLiteNone
Semantic search+ fastembed (ONNX, ~50MB)One-time model download
Vector search+ sqlite-vec (pip installable)None
LLM consolidation+ ctransformers + ~600MB GGUFOne-time model download, or remote API

Hindsight Self-Hosted

ComponentRequirement
API serverDocker + FastAPI + uvicorn
DatabasePostgreSQL + pgvector
Embeddingssentence-transformers or TEI container
LLMYour own API key (OpenAI, Groq, Ollama, etc.)
Control planeNext.js UI on port 9999

Benchmarks: The Truth

Mnemosyne's published "0.076ms vs 38ms" comparison compares in-process SQLite to HTTP API roundtrips. That is not apples-to-apples for semantic retrieval.

Fairer framing:

MetricMnemosyne (in-process)Hindsight Self-Hosted (local HTTP)
Raw SQLite SELECT~0.1msN/A (PostgreSQL over IPC)
Semantic recall (10K corpus)~2-10ms~50-200ms
Memory footprint~100MB~500MB+
Deployment complexitypip installDocker Compose

Migration Path

From Hindsight Self-Hosted to Mnemosyne:

Not a drop-in replacement. Mnemosyne lacks:

  • Entity resolution
  • Automatic fact extraction
  • Cross-machine API
  • Multi-tenancy

From Mnemosyne to Hindsight Self-Hosted:

Export Mnemosyne data:

from mnemosyne import Mnemosyne
mem = Mnemosyne()
mem.export_to_file("mnemosyne-backup.json")

Then adapt to Hindsight's retain API. Structured fact extraction will need to be re-run.

Summary

MnemosyneHindsight Self-Hosted
What it isA memory layerA memory engine
ComplexityLowHigh
Magic levelLow (you control everything)High (LLM extracts, resolves, consolidates)
Best forBuilders who want controlUsers who want sophistication
Honest verdictReliable and dumbSmart and complex

They are different points on the simplicity-sophistication spectrum. Choose based on which trade-offs you prefer.
